attempts.rationale: Resolution boundary: this resolves true only if SpaceX completed at least 100 orbital launches from 2024-01-01 through 2024-12-31 UTC. The reference class is SpaceX annual orbital-launch totals following a year of high cadence and strong recent growth, especially cases where the next-year target requires only low single-digit percentage growth. From the fixed packet, SpaceX rose from 31 launches in 2021 to 61 in 2022 to 96 in 2023, so a 100-launch 2024 outcome requires only about 4.2% growth over 2023. A simple base-rate read from the limited recent history says continuation above the prior record is more likely than not, but sustaining record operations for a full year has meaningful failure modes. I start around 68% because the recent trend is strongly upward and the threshold is close to the prior year, then update upward because late-2023 cadence was already near twice weekly, which annualizes to roughly the required pace. I do not go higher because grounding, range, weather, pad, customer, and attention risks are correlated and could remove enough launch opportunities to miss by a small margin.
attempts.strongest_yes: The target is barely above the 2023 total: after 96 launches and a late-2023 cadence near twice weekly, SpaceX only needed to maintain roughly existing operations with minimal growth.
attempts.strongest_no: Because the target is close to the run-rate, a single meaningful Falcon 9 grounding or several compounding range/weather/pad delays could push the total below 100 despite strong demand and capability.
attempts.key_uncertainties: ["Frequency and duration of any Falcon 9 grounding or anomaly investigation in 2024.","Whether range and pad capacity can support near twice-weekly operations throughout the year.","How many Starlink missions SpaceX can internally schedule without payload, manufacturing, or operational bottlenecks.","Whether customer delays materially reduce non-Starlink launch count.","Whether Starship-related focus materially affects Falcon 9 operations."]
attempts.premortem: If the forecast is wrong and SpaceX does not reach 100, the most likely explanation is not lack of ambition but a temporary Falcon 9 grounding, pad/range bottleneck, weather clustering, or operational friction that removes several launch slots. Because 100 is only slightly above 2023, a modest disruption could be enough to miss.
attempts.wildcards: ["Major launch vehicle anomaly causing a prolonged stand-down.","Unexpected range or pad outage at a key launch site.","Regulatory or operational bottleneck affecting high-cadence launches.","Large acceleration in Starlink deployment demand or operational automation that pushes cadence well above twice weekly."]

attempts.rationale: Resolution boundary: this resolves true only if SpaceX completed at least 100 orbital launches from 2024-01-01 00:00 UTC through 2024-12-31 23:59 UTC. The threshold is only 4 more launches than the 96 completed in 2023, requiring roughly 4.2% growth, not another step-change like 2021 to 2022 or 2022 to 2023. My reference class is high-cadence operational programs that just set a record year and need low-single-digit growth the next year while retaining the same core vehicle, pads, and demand driver. I start around 60% because sustaining a record industrial cadence is meaningfully hard, but modest year-over-year growth after demonstrated acceleration is more likely than not. I update upward because the recent trajectory is very strong: 31 to 61 to 96 launches, with late-2023 cadence already near twice weekly and Starlink providing a large internal demand source less dependent on external customer timing. I do not go above the mid/high 70s because the margin over 100 is small in the opposite direction too: a Falcon 9 grounding, range bottlenecks, pad issues, weather clusters, or operational attention diverted to Starship could easily erase a few launches. Net: the case-specific evidence makes 100+ launches likely, but not close to certain.
attempts.strongest_yes: The 100-launch threshold is very close to the already-achieved 2023 level of 96, and the same Starlink-driven Falcon 9 cadence had already reached roughly twice weekly by late 2023, so only modest operational continuation plus slight improvement is needed.
attempts.strongest_no: The forecast depends on uninterrupted execution of a record launch tempo across an entire year; a single grounding, pad/range constraint, or run of weather and scheduling slips could erase the small 4-launch margin needed to cross 100.

A secondary false scenario is that Starship-related attention and operational complexity slow Falcon cadence enough that 2024 roughly matches but does not exceed 2023.

The fixed evidence says SpaceX reached 96 orbital launches in 2023 after rapid growth from 61 in 2022 and 31 in 2021, with late-2023 cadence already near twice weekly. I pull the final probability down from the inside view because the threshold leaves limited slack: a Falcon 9 grounding, range or pad bottleneck, weather clustering, customer delays, or management/engineering attention diverted to Starship could easily cost several launches. Overall, the evidence favors yes, but not overwhelmingly because sustaining a record operational cadence for a full year is fragile.

There is also resolution risk around what counts as an orbital launch and UTC calendar-year timing, but the criteria are fairly clear: completed orbital launches only, in 2024 UTC. attempts.rationale: Resolution boundary: this resolves true only if the UK general election polling day falls between 2024-01-01 and 2024-12-31 inclusive; it resolves false if polling day is in January 2025 or otherwise outside calendar year 2024. My reference class is discretionary election timing cases where a parliament is entering the final year before a hard legal deadline. Even without detailed historical counts in the evidence packet, the structural base rate is high because almost all remaining legal time is in 2024 and a non-2024 outcome requires using the latest possible timetable. I set the base rate at 85%, allowing for strategic delay by an unpopular incumbent. Case-specific evidence pushes upward: the packet says a 2024 election was broadly expected, waiting until January 2025 would reduce flexibility and create political risk, and possible timings spanned spring, autumn, or very late 2024. The main downward update is that Conservatives were trailing badly, giving Sunak an incentive to delay in hopes conditions improved. Net: very likely but not certain, with most uncertainty concentrated in whether the government deliberately waits until the final January 2025 window.
attempts.strongest_yes: The strongest yes argument is that the legal deadline was January 2025, nearly all practical remaining election-timing options were in 2024, and the packet explicitly says a 2024 election was broadly expected while a non-2024 result required using the latest possible timetable.
attempts.strongest_no: The strongest no argument is that a badly trailing incumbent with control over timing might rationally delay as long as legally possible, accepting the reduced flexibility of a January 2025 election to maximize time for polling or economic improvement.
attempts.key_uncertainties: ["How much value Sunak would place on extra time versus the operational and political risk of the latest possible timetable.","Whether late-2023 polling weakness made delay more attractive or made an earlier reset unavoidable.","Whether practical constraints around campaign timing made January 2025 meaningfully less feasible than merely legally possible.","The evidence packet lacks a quantified historical frequency for similar UK election-timing situations."]
attempts.premortem: If this forecast is wrong, the most likely reason is that I underweighted the incumbent's incentive to delay while badly behind in the polls. Sunak could choose the latest possible timetable because any earlier election looked likely to lose, making January 2025 preferable despite reduced flexibility. Another failure mode is treating 'broadly expected' as too predictive when expectations may reflect conventional wisdom rather than binding constraints.
attempts.wildcards: ["Sudden improvement or deterioration in Conservative polling could shift incentives toward a specific election window.","Unexpected political crisis could force or discourage an earlier election.","Administrative or parliamentary-calendar constraints could make the latest timetable less or more attractive than assumed."]

A simple base rate from the timing window is already high: on 2024-01-01, nearly all practical election dates before the maximum deadline fall in calendar 2024, while a false resolution requires pushing the timetable to the latest possible January 2025 path. attempts.rationale: Resolution boundary: this resolves true only if Apple publicly released an iPhone model with a foldable display before 2026-01-01, not merely if it announced research, filed patents, or released another foldable/non-iPhone device. My reference class is major consumer-electronics incumbents entering an already commercialized but technically difficult smartphone hardware form factor within one calendar year when, at the start of the year, there is no firm public launch signal and rumor timing points later. I estimate that base rate around 15%: launches can happen after years of private development, but absence of a public signal inside a one-year window is a strong negative. The case-specific evidence moves this down because the packet says Apple had repeatedly delayed or avoided the category, analyst notes pointed more to 2026 or later, and Apple prioritizes durability, crease quality, software polish, and margins. The remaining probability is not zero because foldables already existed commercially and Apple was reportedly exploring displays and hinges, so a surprise 2025 product-cycle release is possible but unlikely.
attempts.strongest_yes: The strongest yes argument is that commercial foldables already existed and Apple was reportedly exploring foldable displays and hinges, so a 2025 release could emerge from private development even without a public signal on January 1, 2025.
attempts.strongest_no: The strongest no argument is that the evidence packet combines no announcement, repeated delay or avoidance, Apple-specific caution on hardware quality and margins, and rumor timing centered on 2026 or later, all of which point against a release before 2026-01-01.
attempts.key_uncertainties: ["How often Apple-level private development produces a release within one year despite no firm public launch signal.","Whether analyst notes pointing to 2026 or later reflect actual supply-chain timing or conservative public rumor lag.","Whether Apple would define a foldable iPhone as ready despite durability, crease, software, and margin concerns."]
attempts.premortem: If this forecast is wrong, the most likely reason is that Apple had already solved the key hardware and margin issues before 2025, kept the launch unusually quiet, and used the 2025 iPhone cycle to release a foldable model despite external reports pointing later.
attempts.wildcards: ["A strategic decision to enter foldables earlier to counter Samsung and Chinese vendors.","A supply-chain or display breakthrough that made durability and crease quality acceptable sooner than expected.","A limited or premium release that qualified as public release even if volumes were constrained."]

cases.question: As of January 1, 2024, will the US Federal Reserve cut the federal funds target range at least once in calendar year 2024?

output.rationale: Aggregated 3 differentiated but correlated forecaster probabilities with a median. Mean=76.7, median=76, disagreement=6. Review component attempts for base-rate quality, resolution-boundary handling, and correlated calibration warnings.

attempts.rationale: Resolution boundary: this resolves true if the FOMC lowers the federal funds target range at least once between 2024-01-01 and 2024-12-31, at either a scheduled or unscheduled meeting. I use a reference class of calendar years after an aggressive Fed hiking cycle when policy is already restrictive and Fed communications or projections have shifted toward easing. Without external historical counts, I estimate that base rate at about 65% for at least one cut during the following year. The case-specific packet pushes above that base rate because the target range is high at 5.25%-5.50%, inflation has cooled substantially, December 2023 FOMC projections imply multiple 2024 cuts conditional on continued cooling, and market pricing plus communications had shifted toward cuts as a plausible base case. The main offset is that inflation remains above target and labor markets are resilient, so cuts could be delayed or canceled if inflation reaccelerates. Net: a cut sometime in 2024 is more likely than not, with timing uncertainty larger than direction uncertainty.
attempts.strongest_yes: The strongest yes argument is that the Fed itself, through December 2023 projections, had already signaled multiple 2024 cuts under continued disinflation, and inflation had already cooled substantially from its peak while policy was restrictive.
attempts.strongest_no: The strongest no argument is that inflation was still above target and a resilient labor market could let the Fed keep rates high all year, especially if inflation reaccelerated.
attempts.key_uncertainties: ["Whether inflation would continue cooling toward target during 2024.","Whether labor-market resilience would keep demand and wage pressure too strong for cuts.","Whether the Fed would prioritize avoiding premature easing over responding to declining inflation.","Whether market pricing and communications were overinterpreting conditional FOMC projections."]
attempts.premortem: If this forecast resolves false, the likely explanation is that inflation reaccelerated or stalled above target, resilient labor conditions reduced pressure to ease, and the FOMC judged that cutting before clear inflation convergence would risk reigniting price pressures. Another failure mode is over-weighting conditional projections that depended on continued cooling rather than treating them as contingent scenarios.
attempts.wildcards: ["A renewed inflation shock that forces rates to stay unchanged all year.","A sudden growth or financial-stability shock that could force earlier or larger cuts than expected.","A labor-market weakening that rapidly shifts the Fed from patience to easing."]
attempts.cited_sources: [{"claim":"Fixed evidence packet states that the federal funds target range was 5.25% to 5.50%, inflation had cooled but remained above target, December 2023 FOMC projections implied multiple 2024 cuts if cooling continued, labor markets were resilient, and market pricing plus Fed communications had shifted toward cuts as a plausible base case."}]
attempts.reference_class: Calendar years following an aggressive Federal Reserve hiking cycle with a high/restrictive federal funds target range and official communications or projections shifting toward possible easing within the next year.
attempts.resolution_boundary: True if the FOMC lowered the federal funds target range at least once at any scheduled or unscheduled meeting from 2024-01-01 through 2024-12-31; false if the target range was never lowered during calendar year 2024.

attempts.rationale: Resolution boundary: this resolves true if the FOMC lowers the federal funds target range at least once between 2024-01-01 and 2024-12-31, regardless of whether the cut occurs at a scheduled or unscheduled meeting. My base rate is 65% for years following an aggressive hiking cycle where policy is already restrictive and inflation is declining but still above target: the central bank often has room and incentive to ease within the next calendar year, but a full-year hold remains common if inflation persistence or labor strength blocks confidence. I update upward by about 10 points because the December 2023 FOMC projections already implied multiple 2024 cuts conditional on continued cooling, meaning cuts were not just a market hope but inside the Fed's own modal policy path. I update upward another 5 points because the target range was high at 5.25%-5.50%, creating a meaningful buffer to cut without becoming accommodative. I subtract around 3 points for resilient labor markets, which could delay cuts, and 2-4 points for inflation still being above target and the explicit risk of reacceleration. Net: the evidence packet points to cuts as the central/plausible base case, with the main blocker being inflation persistence strong enough to keep the Fed on hold all year. Because the question only requires at least one cut across the whole calendar year, not multiple cuts or early cuts, the threshold distance is moderate-to-low: even delayed confidence by mid or late 2024 could still satisfy the resolution.
attempts.strongest_yes: The strongest yes argument is that official December 2023 FOMC projections already implied multiple cuts in 2024, and the question only asks for at least one cut, so the Fed would need to abandon or postpone its own easing path for the entire year for this to resolve false.
attempts.strongest_no: The strongest no argument is that inflation was still above target and labor markets were resilient, so if inflation reaccelerated or stopped cooling, the Fed could credibly hold the 5.25%-5.50% range all year to preserve anti-inflation credibility.
attempts.key_uncertainties: ["Whether inflation continues cooling enough for the Fed to gain confidence before the final 2024 meetings.","Whether resilient labor markets keep wage and demand pressures too strong for cuts.","Whether Fed officials prioritize avoiding premature easing over normalizing from a restrictive level.","Whether market pricing and FOMC projections reflect genuine policy intent or are highly conditional on favorable data.","How much inflation reacceleration would be enough to block even a single late-year cut."]
attempts.premortem: If this forecast is wrong and no cut occurs in 2024, the likely story is that inflation either reaccelerated or proved sticky enough that the Fed judged any cut premature, while labor market resilience removed pressure to ease. In that world, December 2023 projections were highly conditional rather than a firm signal, and I overweighted the high starting rate and underweighted the Fed's credibility constraint around above-target inflation.

attempts.rationale: Resolution is true if the FOMC lowers the federal funds target range at least once between 2024-01-01 and 2024-12-31. Starting from a reference class of years following an aggressive hiking cycle where policy is restrictive, inflation is cooling, and official projections imply easing, the base rate favors at least one cut but not certainty. The fixed evidence points toward cuts: the target range is high at 5.25%-5.50%, inflation has declined substantially, December 2023 FOMC projections imply multiple 2024 cuts conditional on continued cooling, and market pricing plus Fed communications have shifted toward cuts as a plausible base case. The main counterweight is that inflation remains above target and labor markets are resilient, so the Fed could hold all year if inflation reaccelerates or progress stalls. Netting these, at least one cut in the whole calendar year is more likely than not, with timing uncertainty less important because the resolution only requires one cut by year-end.

The resolution also ignores how close the Fed comes to cutting: even strong signals, dovish projections, or cuts expected just after 2024 would still resolve false if no target-range reduction occurs by 2024-12-31. Another failure mode is correlated evidence: FOMC projections, market pricing, and Fed communications may all reflect the same inflation-cooling assumption, so they are not three independent supports.
